source_title: Irreparable tears: SCR and other salvage options
topic: Irreparable tears: SCR and other salvage options
summary: For massive/irreparable posterosuperior cuff tears, options include superior capsular reconstruction (SCR) with dermal allograft, fascia lata autograft/allograft, or long head of biceps; subacromial balloon spacer; biologic tuberoplasty; tendon transfer; and reverse shoulder arthroplasty. Biomechanical studies compare graft choices (fascia lata vs single/double-layer dermal allograft) but evidence is heterogeneous and outcomes are technique- and patient-dependent. Vendors differentiate by which salvage technology they champion: dermal allograft SCR (Arthrex) vs balloon spacer (Stryker InSpace) vs biologic-healing framing (S&N).
arthrex_position: Arthrex strongly promotes arthroscopic SCR with ArthroFLEX acellular dermal allograft (and knotless glenoid/greater-tuberosity anchor techniques), positioning SCR as a joint-preserving alternative to reverse arthroplasty in younger patients.
stryker_position: Stryker's signature irreparable-tear product is the InSpace subacromial balloon spacer (FDA-approved 2021 for patients >65; shown non-inferior to partial repair in its pivotal data), marketed as a fast, implant-only alternative to SCR.
smithnephew_position: Smith & Nephew leans on biologic healing (REGENETEN augmentation, REGENESORB) and dermal allograft/patch options rather than a proprietary balloon; its irreparable-tear messaging centers on inducing/strengthening native tissue and standard graft reconstruction.
rep_talk_track: An Arthrex rep honestly pitches dermal allograft SCR as joint-preserving with growing biomechanical and clinical support, while acknowledging it's technically demanding. A Stryker rep pitches InSpace as a far simpler, quicker, implant-only option with FDA approval and non-inferiority data vs partial repair - while honestly noting it's indicated mainly for older patients. An S&N rep reframes toward augmenting reparable tissue and biologic healing before resorting to salvage. The honest disclaimer across all three: comparative evidence among SCR graft types, balloon spacer, and arthroplasty is still maturing and largely not head-to-head, so technique choice is patient-specific, not a settled 'best' device.
evidence: Arthrex describes all-arthroscopic SCR with ArthroFLEX 2-6 mm acellular dermal allograft; biomechanical studies compare fascia lata vs single/double-layer dermal allograft with mixed conclusions.
InSpace subacromial balloon (Stryker): FDA approval 2021 for patients >65; pivotal data reported non-inferior to partial rotator cuff repair.
Biologic tuberoplasty and biceps-based SCR described as additional salvage options in the literature.
text:
Irreparable tears: SCR and other salvage options

For massive/irreparable posterosuperior cuff tears, options include superior capsular reconstruction (SCR) with dermal allograft, fascia lata autograft/allograft, or long head of biceps; subacromial balloon spacer; biologic tuberoplasty; tendon transfer; and reverse shoulder arthroplasty. Biomechanical studies compare graft choices (fascia lata vs single/double-layer dermal allograft) but evidence is heterogeneous and outcomes are technique- and patient-dependent. Vendors differentiate by which salvage technology they champion: dermal allograft SCR (Arthrex) vs balloon spacer (Stryker InSpace) vs biologic-healing framing (S&N).

Arthrex: Arthrex strongly promotes arthroscopic SCR with ArthroFLEX acellular dermal allograft (and knotless glenoid/greater-tuberosity anchor techniques), positioning SCR as a joint-preserving alternative to reverse arthroplasty in younger patients.
Stryker: Stryker's signature irreparable-tear product is the InSpace subacromial balloon spacer (FDA-approved 2021 for patients >65; shown non-inferior to partial repair in its pivotal data), marketed as a fast, implant-only alternative to SCR.
Smith & Nephew: Smith & Nephew leans on biologic healing (REGENETEN augmentation, REGENESORB) and dermal allograft/patch options rather than a proprietary balloon; its irreparable-tear messaging centers on inducing/strengthening native tissue and standard graft reconstruction.

Rep positioning: An Arthrex rep honestly pitches dermal allograft SCR as joint-preserving with growing biomechanical and clinical support, while acknowledging it's technically demanding. A Stryker rep pitches InSpace as a far simpler, quicker, implant-only option with FDA approval and non-inferiority data vs partial repair - while honestly noting it's indicated mainly for older patients. An S&N rep reframes toward augmenting reparable tissue and biologic healing before resorting to salvage. The honest disclaimer across all three: comparative evidence among SCR graft types, balloon spacer, and arthroplasty is still maturing and largely not head-to-head, so technique choice is patient-specific, not a settled 'best' device.

Evidence: (1) Arthrex describes all-arthroscopic SCR with ArthroFLEX 2-6 mm acellular dermal allograft; biomechanical studies compare fascia lata vs single/double-layer dermal allograft with mixed conclusions. (2) InSpace subacromial balloon (Stryker): FDA approval 2021 for patients >65; pivotal data reported non-inferior to partial rotator cuff repair. (3) Biologic tuberoplasty and biceps-based SCR described as additional salvage options in the literature.
